AI.motivation: The primary tools used to monitor and defend object detectors under adversarial attack assume that when accuracy degrades, detection count drops in tandem, but this assumption has not been measured.
AI.method: The study tests four SNN architectures and two threat models (l-infinity and l-2) on a single model, EMS-YOLO, and evaluates the performance of five standard defense components.
AI.result: The results show that EMS-YOLO retains more than 70% of its detections while mAP collapses from 0.528 to 0.042 under standard PGD, and that all five standard defense components fail to detect or mitigate QC.
AI.conclusion: The study provides evidence that adversarial failure modes can be substrate-dependent, and that the defense ecosystem may rely on a shared assumption calibrated on a single substrate, highlighting the need for further research into QC and its mitigation.

title: To Memorize or to Retrieve: Scaling Laws for RAG-Considerate Pretraining
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves language model (LM) performance by providing relevant context at test time for knowledge-intensive situations. However, the relationship between parametric knowledge acquired during pretraining and non-parametric knowledge accessed via retrieval remains poorly understood, especially under fixed data budgets. In this work, we systematically study the trade-off between pretraining corpus size and retrieval store size across a wide range of model and data scales. We train OLMo-2-based LMs ranging from 30M to 3B parameters on up to 100B tokens of DCLM data, while varying both pretraining data scale (1-150x the number of parameters) and retrieval store size (1-20x), and evaluate performance across a diverse suite of benchmarks spanning reasoning, scientific QA, and open-domain QA. We find that retrieval consistently improves performance over parametric-only baselines across model scales and introduce a three-dimensional scaling framework that models performance as a function of model size, pretraining tokens, and retrieval corpus size. This scaling manifold enables us to estimate optimal allocations of a fixed data budget between pretraining and retrieval, revealing that the marginal utility of retrieval depends strongly on model scale, task type, and the degree of pretraining saturation. Our results provide a quantitative foundation for understanding when and how retrieval should complement pretraining, offering practical guidance for allocating data resources in the design of scalable language modeling systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the trade-off between pretraining corpus size and retrieval store size for language models, finding that retrieval consistently improves performance and providing a framework for optimal data budget allocation.
AI.motivation: The relationship between parametric knowledge acquired during pretraining and non-parametric knowledge accessed via retrieval is poorly understood, especially under fixed data budgets.
AI.method: The authors train OLMo-2-based LMs with varying pretraining data scales and retrieval store sizes, evaluating performance across diverse benchmarks.
AI.result: Retrieval consistently improves performance over parametric-only baselines across model scales, and a three-dimensional scaling framework is introduced to estimate optimal data budget allocations.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a quantitative foundation for understanding when and how retrieval should complement pretraining, offering practical guidance for allocating data resources in scalable language modeling systems.

title: TALENT: Target-aware Efficient Tuning for Referring Image Segmentation
summary: Referring image segmentation aims to segment specific targets based on a natural text expression. Recently, parameter-efficient tuning (PET) has emerged as a promising paradigm. However, existing PET-based methods often suffer from the fact that visual features can't emphasize the text-referred target instance but activate co-category yet unrelated objects. We analyze and quantify this problem, terming it the `non-target activation' (NTA) issue. To address this, we propose a novel framework, TALENT, which utilizes target-aware efficient tuning for PET-based RIS. Specifically, we first propose a Rectified Cost Aggregator (RCA) to efficiently aggregate text-referred features. Then, to calibrate `NTA' into accurate target activation, we adopt a Target-aware Learning Mechanism (TLM), including contextual pairwise consistency learning and target-centric contrastive learning. The former uses the sentence-level text feature to achieve a holistic understanding of the referent and constructs a text-referred affinity map to optimize the semantic association of visual features. The latter further enhances target localization to discover the distinct instance while suppressing associations with other unrelated ones. The two objectives work in concert and address `NTA' effectively. Extensive evaluations show that TALENT outperforms existing methods across various metrics (e.g., 2.5\% mIoU gains on G-Ref val set). Our codes will be released at: https://github.com/Kimsure/TALENT.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TALENT, a novel framework for Referring Image Segmentation (RIS) using target-aware efficient tuning, addressing the 'non-target activation' issue in existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing PET-based RIS methods suffer from 'non-target activation' (NTA), where visual features activate co-category but unrelated objects instead of the text-referred target instance.
AI.method: The proposed TALENT framework utilizes a Rectified Cost Aggregator (RCA) and a Target-aware Learning Mechanism (TLM), which includes contextual pairwise consistency learning and target-centric contrastive learning.
AI.result: TALENT outperforms existing methods across various metrics, achieving 2.5% mIoU gains on the G-Ref val set.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TALENT framework effectively addresses the 'non-target activation' issue in RIS, leading to improved performance over existing methods.

title: LangMARL: Natural Language Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents struggle to autonomously evolve coordination strategies in dynamic environments, largely because coarse global outcomes obscure the causal signals needed for local policy refinement. We identify this bottleneck as a multi-agent credit assignment problem, which has long been studied in classical multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) but remains underaddressed in LLM-based systems. Building on this observation, we propose LangMARL, a framework that brings credit assignment and policy gradient evolution from cooperative MARL into the language space. LangMARL introduces agent-level language credit assignment, pioneers gradient evolution in language space for policy improvement, and summarizes task-relevant causal relations from replayed trajectories to provide dense feedback and improve convergence under sparse rewards. Extensive experiments across diverse cooperative multi-agent tasks demonstrate improved sample efficiency, interpretability, and strong generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LangMARL, a framework that applies multi-agent reinforcement learning to large language models to improve coordination in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Large language model agents struggle to evolve coordination strategies in dynamic environments due to a multi-agent credit assignment problem.
AI.method: The authors propose LangMARL, which brings credit assignment and policy gradient evolution from cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning into the language space.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate improved sample efficiency, interpretability, and strong generalization across diverse cooperative multi-agent tasks.
AI.conclusion: LangMARL improves the ability of large language model agents to autonomously evolve coordination strategies in dynamic environments.

title: DirectFisheye-GS: Enabling Native Fisheye Input in Gaussian Splatting with Cross-View Joint Optimization
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has enabled efficient 3D scene reconstruction from everyday images with real-time, high-fidelity rendering, greatly advancing VR/AR applications. Fisheye cameras, with their wider field of view (FOV), promise high-quality reconstructions from fewer inputs and have recently attracted much attention. However, since 3DGS relies on rasterization, most subsequent works involving fisheye camera inputs first undistort images before training, which introduces two problems: 1) Black borders at image edges cause information loss and negate the fisheye's large FOV advantage; 2) Undistortion's stretch-and-interpolate resampling spreads each pixel's value over a larger area, diluting detail density -- causes 3DGS overfitting these low-frequency zones, producing blur and floating artifacts. In this work, we integrate fisheye camera model into the original 3DGS framework, enabling native fisheye image input for training without preprocessing. Despite correct modeling, we observed that the reconstructed scenes still exhibit floaters at image edges: Distortion increases toward the periphery, and 3DGS's original per-iteration random-selecting-view optimization ignores the cross-view correlations of a Gaussian, leading to extreme shapes (e.g., oversized or elongated) that degrade reconstruction quality. To address this, we introduce a feature-overlap-driven cross-view joint optimization strategy that establishes consistent geometric and photometric constraints across views-a technique equally applicable to existing pinhole-camera-based pipelines. Our DirectFisheye-GS matches or surpasses state-of-the-art performance on public datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DirectFisheye-GS, a method to integrate fisheye camera inputs into 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) framework without preprocessing, achieving state-of-the-art performance in 3D scene reconstruction.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of current 3DGS methods when using fisheye cameras, which have a wider field of view but introduce distortions that degrade reconstruction quality.
AI.method: The method involves integrating the fisheye camera model into the original 3DGS framework and introducing a feature-overlap-driven cross-view joint optimization strategy to establish consistent geometric and photometric constraints across views.
AI.result: The result is that DirectFisheye-GS matches or surpasses state-of-the-art performance on public datasets, producing high-quality 3D scene reconstructions from fisheye camera inputs.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the proposed DirectFisheye-GS method enables efficient and high-fidelity 3D scene reconstruction from fisheye camera inputs, advancing VR/AR applications.

title: Stochastic Attention: Connectome-Inspired Randomized Routing for Expressive Linear-Time Attention
summary: The whole-brain connectome of a fruit fly comprises over 130K neurons connected with a probability of merely 0.02%, yet achieves an average shortest path of only 4.4 hops. Despite being highly structured at the circuit level, the network's long-range connections are broadly distributed across brain regions, functioning as stochastic shortcuts that enable efficient global communication. Inspired by this observation, we propose Stochastic Attention (SA), a drop-in enhancement for sliding-window attention (SWA) that applies a random permutation to the token sequence before windowed attention and restores the original order afterward. This transforms the fixed local window into a stochastic global one within the same $O(nw)$ per-layer budget. Through depth, independently sampled permutations yield exponentially growing receptive fields, achieving full sequence coverage in $O(\log_w n)$ layers versus $O(n/w)$ for SWA. We validate SA in two settings: pre-training language models from scratch, where a gated SA + SWA combination achieves the best average zero-shot accuracy, and training-free inference on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-30B-A3B, where SA consistently outperforms SWA and matches or exceeds Mixture of Block Attention at comparable compute budgets. These results suggest that connectome-inspired stochastic routing is a practical primitive for improving the expressivity of efficient attention, complementary to existing linear and sparse approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Stochastic Attention (SA), a method inspired by the fruit fly's brain connectome, which applies random permutations to the token sequence in sliding-window attention to enable efficient global communication.
AI.motivation: The fruit fly's brain connectome achieves efficient global communication with a highly structured circuit level and broadly distributed long-range connections, inspiring the development of SA.
AI.method: Stochastic Attention (SA) applies a random permutation to the token sequence before windowed attention and restores the original order afterward, transforming the fixed local window into a stochastic global one.
AI.result: SA outperforms SWA and matches or exceeds Mixture of Block Attention at comparable compute budgets in pre-training language models and training-free inference on large models.
AI.conclusion: The results suggest that connectome-inspired stochastic routing is a practical primitive for improving the expressivity of efficient attention, complementary to existing linear and sparse approaches.

title: When AI and Experts Agree on Error: Intrinsic Ambiguity in Dermatoscopic Images
summary: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI), particularly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), into dermatological diagnosis demonstrates substantial clinical potential. While existing literature predominantly benchmarks algorithmic performance against human experts, our study adopts a novel perspective by investigating the intrinsic complexity of dermatoscopic images. Through rigorous experimentation with multiple CNN architectures, we isolated a subset of images systematically misclassified across all models-a phenomenon statistically proven to exceed random chance. To determine if these failures stem from algorithmic biases or inherent visual ambiguity, expert dermatologists independently evaluated these challenging cases alongside a control group. The results revealed a collapse in human diagnostic performance on the AI-misclassified images. First, agreement with ground-truth labels plummeted, with Cohen's kappa dropping to a mere 0.08 for the difficult images, compared to a 0.61 for the control group. Second, we observed a severe deterioration in expert consensus; inter-rater reliability among physicians fell from moderate concordance (Fleiss kappa = 0.456) on control images to only modest agreement (Fleiss kappa = 0.275) on difficult cases. We identified image quality as a primary driver of these dual systematic failures. To promote transparency and reproducibility, all data, code, and trained models have been made publicly available

AI.tldr: The study investigates the complexity of dermatoscopic images and the limitations of AI and human experts in diagnosing skin conditions.
AI.motivation: To understand the intrinsic complexity of dermatoscopic images and the challenges faced by AI and human experts in diagnosing skin conditions.
AI.method: The study used multiple CNN architectures to identify a subset of images that were systematically misclassified. Expert dermatologists then evaluated these challenging cases alongside a control group.
AI.result: The study found that human diagnostic performance deteriorated significantly on AI-misclassified images, with a collapse in agreement with ground-truth labels and expert consensus.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the importance of image quality in dermatological diagnosis and the need for transparency and reproducibility in AI research.

title: From Baselines to Preferences: A Comparative Study of LoRA/QLoRA and Preference Optimization for Mental Health Text Classification
summary: Mental health text classification has rapidly adopted modern adaptation methods, yet practical guidance on which optimization strategy to use, when, and why remains limited. This paper presents a systematic comparative study of optimization pathways for a joint mental-health classification task, moving from strong vanilla baselines to progressively more specialized techniques. We first establish classical and encoder references, then examine parameter-efficient supervised fine-tuning with LoRA/QLoRA under multiple objective and optimization settings, and finally evaluate preference-based optimization with DPO, ORPO, and KTO, including class-rebalanced training. Rather than emphasizing a single headline score, we focus on methodological insight: how performance changes with objective formulation, adapter choice, optimizer behavior, context windowing, and class-balance intervention. The results show that optimization effects are highly method-dependent: some approaches deliver stable, transferable gains, while others are sensitive to configuration and data balance. Preference optimization, in particular, exhibits large variation across objectives, indicating that method selection is more consequential than simply adding a preference-training stage. The central contribution is a clear optimization narrative for mental health NLP: start from transparent baselines, apply controlled tuning, and use preference optimization selectively where its gains are demonstrable. This provides a reproducible and practically grounded framework for choosing effective training strategies beyond architecture choice alone.

AI.tldr: The paper compares optimization strategies for mental health text classification, finding that method selection significantly impacts performance and that preference optimization should be used selectively.
AI.motivation: The lack of practical guidance on choosing optimization strategies for mental health text classification.
AI.method: A systematic comparative study of optimization pathways, including supervised fine-tuning with LoRA/QLoRA and preference-based optimization with DPO, ORPO, and KTO.
AI.result: Optimization effects are highly method-dependent, and some approaches deliver stable gains while others are sensitive to configuration and data balance.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a clear optimization narrative for mental health NLP, recommending a framework that starts with transparent baselines, applies controlled tuning, and uses preference optimization selectively.

title: CL-VISTA: Benchmarking Continual Learning in Video Large Language Models
summary: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) require continual learning to adapt to non-stationary real-world data. However, existing benchmarks fall short of evaluating modern foundation models: many still rely on models without large-scale pre-training, and prevailing benchmarks typically partition a single dataset into sub-tasks, resulting in high task redundancy and negligible forgetting on pre-trained Video-LLMs. To address these limitations, we propose CL-VISTA, a benchmark tailored for continual video understanding of Video-LLMs. By curating 8 diverse tasks spanning perception, understanding, and reasoning, CL-VISTA induces substantial distribution shifts that effectively expose catastrophic forgetting. To systematically assess CL methods, we establish a comprehensive evaluation framework comprising 6 distinct protocols across 3 critical dimensions: performance, computational efficiency, and memory footprint. Notably, the performance dimension incorporates a general video understanding assessment to assess whether CL methods genuinely enhance foundational intelligence or merely induce task-specific overfitting. Extensive benchmarking of 10 mainstream CL methods reveals a fundamental trade-off: no single approach achieves universal superiority across all dimensions. Methods that successfully mitigate catastrophic forgetting tend to compromise generalization or incur prohibitive computational and memory overheads. We hope CL-VISTA provides critical insights for advancing continual learning in multimodal foundation models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a benchmark called CL-VISTA for evaluating continual learning in Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs).
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for evaluating Video-LLMs have limitations, such as relying on models without large-scale pre-training and having high task redundancy, which makes it difficult to assess catastrophic forgetting in pre-trained models.
AI.method: The authors propose a comprehensive evaluation framework with 6 protocols across 3 dimensions (performance, computational efficiency, and memory footprint) to assess continual learning methods. They also curate 8 diverse tasks for the CL-VISTA benchmark.
AI.result: The benchmarking of 10 mainstream continual learning methods reveals a trade-off between mitigating catastrophic forgetting, generalization, and computational/memory overheads. No single approach achieves universal superiority across all dimensions.
AI.conclusion: The CL-VISTA benchmark and evaluation framework provide critical insights for advancing continual learning in multimodal foundation models, highlighting the need for a balanced approach that considers multiple dimensions.

title: From Early Encoding to Late Suppression: Interpreting LLMs on Character Counting Tasks
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) exhibit failures on elementary symbolic tasks such as character counting in a word, despite excelling on complex benchmarks. Although this limitation has been noted, the internal reasons remain unclear. We use character counting (e.g., "How many p's are in apple?") as a minimal, controlled probe that isolates token-level reasoning from higher-level confounds. Using this setting, we uncover a consistent phenomenon across modern architectures, including LLaMA, Qwen, and Gemma: models often compute the correct answer internally yet fail to express it at the output layer.
  Through mechanistic analysis combining probing classifiers, activation patching, logit lens analysis, and attention head tracing, we show that character-level information is encoded in early and mid-layer representations. However, this information is attenuated by a small set of components in later layers, especially the penultimate and final layer MLP. We identify these components as negative circuits: subnetworks that downweight correct signals in favor of higher-probability but incorrect outputs.
  Our results lead to two contributions. First, we show that symbolic reasoning failures in LLMs are not due to missing representations or insufficient scale, but arise from structured interference within the model's computation graph. This explains why such errors persist and can worsen under scaling and instruction tuning. Second, we provide evidence that LLM forward passes implement a form of competitive decoding, in which correct and incorrect hypotheses coexist and are dynamically reweighted, with final outputs determined by suppression as much as by amplification.
  These findings carry implications for interpretability and robustness: simple symbolic reasoning exposes weaknesses in modern LLMs, underscoring need for design strategies that ensure information is encoded and reliably used.

AI.tldr: Large language models (LLMs) often fail on simple symbolic tasks like character counting due to structured interference within their computation graph, not lack of representation or scale.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand why large language models (LLMs) fail on elementary symbolic tasks, such as character counting, despite excelling on complex benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors use character counting as a probe to analyze LLMs, applying mechanistic analysis techniques like probing classifiers, activation patching, logit lens analysis, and attention head tracing.
AI.result: The study finds that LLMs often compute the correct answer internally but fail to express it at the output layer due to structured interference from 'negative circuits' that downweight correct signals.
AI.conclusion: The findings imply that LLM failures on symbolic reasoning are due to structured interference, not missing representations or scale, and highlight the need for design strategies ensuring encoded information is reliably used.

title: MoonAnything: A Vision Benchmark with Large-Scale Lunar Supervised Data
summary: Accurate perception of lunar surfaces is critical for modern lunar exploration missions. However, developing robust learning-based perception systems is hindered by the lack of datasets that provide both geometric and photometric supervision. Existing lunar datasets typically lack either geometric ground truth, photometric realism, illumination diversity, or large-scale coverage. In this paper, we introduce MoonAnything, a unified benchmark built on real lunar topography with physically-based rendering, providing the first comprehensive geometric and photometric supervision under diverse illumination with large scale. The benchmark comprises two complementary sub-datasets : i) LunarGeo provides stereo images with corresponding dense depth maps and camera calibration enabling 3D reconstruction and pose estimation; ii) LunarPhoto provides photorealistic images using a spatially-varying BRDF model, along with multi-illumination renderings under real solar configurations, enabling reflectance estimation and illumination-robust perception. Together, these datasets offer over 130K samples with comprehensive supervision. Beyond lunar applications, MoonAnything offers a unique setting and challenging testbed for algorithms under low-textured, high-contrast conditions and applies to other airless celestial bodies and could generalize beyond. We establish baselines using state-of-the-art methods and release the complete dataset along with generation tools to support community extension: https://github.com/clementinegrethen/MoonAnything.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark called MoonAnything is introduced for lunar surface perception, providing comprehensive geometric and photometric supervision for training learning-based systems.
AI.motivation: The lack of datasets with both geometric and photometric supervision hinders the development of robust learning-based perception systems for lunar exploration missions.
AI.method: The MoonAnything benchmark is built on real lunar topography with physically-based rendering, comprising two sub-datasets: LunarGeo for 3D reconstruction and pose estimation, and LunarPhoto for reflectance estimation and illumination-robust perception.
AI.result: The benchmark offers over 130K samples with comprehensive supervision, and baselines are established using state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: MoonAnything provides a unique setting and challenging testbed for algorithms under low-textured, high-contrast conditions, and its applications can generalize beyond lunar exploration to other airless celestial bodies.

title: Valency Classification of Mapudungun Verbal Roots. Established by the language's own morphotactics
summary: In the previous work, a lexical (re)categorisation -- or confirmation of the given category -- of roots identified as verbal was undertaken to determine their original category accurately. Building on this, the present paper offers an account of the valency classification of those Mapudungun roots confirmed to be verbal, using the language's own morphotactics; specifically, by examining the permissible and restricted combinations of various suffixes with roots or verbal stems in the Mapuche verb form. As with all work conducted thus far, the results presented here aim to improve the morphological analyser (Dungupeyum) with all verified findings incorporated into the system. From a theoretical perspective, we also hope to contribute to the recognition and understanding of issues related to the valency of Mapuche verb forms.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the valency classification of Mapudungun roots confirmed to be verbal, using the language's morphotactics to examine suffix combinations.
AI.motivation: To improve the morphological analyzer (Dungupeyum) and contribute to the understanding of Mapuche verb form valency.
AI.method: Examining permissible and restricted combinations of suffixes with roots or verbal stems in Mapudungun.
AI.result: Verified findings on valency classification of Mapudungun verbal roots, to be incorporated into Dungupeyum.
AI.conclusion: The study aims to enhance the morphological analyzer and contribute to the theoretical understanding of Mapuche verb form valency.

title: TP-Seg: Task-Prototype Framework for Unified Medical Lesion Segmentation
summary: Building a unified model with a single set of parameters to efficiently handle diverse types of medical lesion segmentation has become a crucial objective for AI-assisted diagnosis. Existing unified segmentation approaches typically rely on shared encoders across heterogeneous tasks and modalities, which often leads to feature entanglement, gradient interference, and suboptimal lesion discrimination. In this work, we propose TP-Seg, a task-prototype framework for unified medical lesion segmentation. On one hand, the task-conditioned adapter effectively balances shared and task-specific representations through a dual-path expert structure, enabling adaptive feature extraction across diverse medical imaging modalities and lesion types. On the other hand, the prototype-guided task decoder introduces learnable task prototypes as semantic anchors and employs a cross-attention mechanism to achieve fine-grained modeling of task-specific foreground and background semantics. Without bells and whistles, TP-Seg consistently outperforms specialized, general and unified segmentation methods across 8 different medical lesion segmentation tasks covering multiple imaging modalities, demonstrating strong generalization, scalability and clinical applicability.

AI.tldr: TP-Seg, a task-prototype framework, is proposed for unified medical lesion segmentation, outperforming existing methods across 8 tasks and multiple imaging modalities.
AI.motivation: The goal is to build a unified model that can efficiently handle diverse types of medical lesion segmentation, addressing issues in existing approaches such as feature entanglement and suboptimal lesion discrimination.
AI.method: The proposed TP-Seg framework consists of a task-conditioned adapter with a dual-path expert structure and a prototype-guided task decoder with learnable task prototypes and a cross-attention mechanism.
AI.result: TP-Seg consistently outperforms specialized, general, and unified segmentation methods across 8 different medical lesion segmentation tasks, demonstrating strong generalization, scalability, and clinical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TP-Seg framework offers a effective solution for unified medical lesion segmentation, showcasing its potential for AI-assisted diagnosis.

title: Emotion Entanglement and Bayesian Inference for Multi-Dimensional Emotion Understanding
summary: Understanding emotions in natural language is inherently a multi-dimensional reasoning problem, where multiple affective signals interact through context, interpersonal relations, and situational cues. However, most existing emotion understanding benchmarks rely on short texts and predefined emotion labels, reducing this process to independent label prediction and ignoring the structured dependencies among emotions. To address this limitation, we introduce Emotional Scenarios (EmoScene), a theory-grounded benchmark of 4,731 context-rich scenarios annotated with an 8-dimensional emotion vector derived from Plutchik's basic emotions. We evaluate six instruction-tuned large language models in a zero-shot setting and observe modest performance, with the best model achieving a Macro F1 of 0.501, highlighting the difficulty of context-aware multi-label emotion prediction. Motivated by the observation that emotions rarely occur independently, we further propose an entanglement-aware Bayesian inference framework that incorporates emotion co-occurrence statistics to perform joint posterior inference over the emotion vector. This lightweight post-processing improves structural consistency of predictions and yields notable gains for weaker models (e.g., +0.051 Macro F1 for Qwen2.5-7B). EmoScene therefore provides a challenging benchmark for studying multi-dimensional emotion understanding and the limitations of current language models.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark called Emotional Scenarios (EmoScene) is introduced to improve emotion understanding in natural language, focusing on context-rich scenarios and multi-dimensional emotion prediction.
AI.motivation: Current emotion understanding benchmarks have limitations, relying on short texts and predefined emotion labels, ignoring structured dependencies among emotions.
AI.method: The EmoScene benchmark is proposed, consisting of 4,731 context-rich scenarios annotated with an 8-dimensional emotion vector, and an entanglement-aware Bayesian inference framework is introduced to incorporate emotion co-occurrence statistics.
AI.result: Six instruction-tuned large language models were evaluated in a zero-shot setting, achieving modest performance, with the best model reaching a Macro F1 of 0.501, and the proposed framework improving structural consistency of predictions.
AI.conclusion: EmoScene provides a challenging benchmark for studying multi-dimensional emotion understanding, highlighting the difficulty of context-aware multi-label emotion prediction and the limitations of current language models.

title: TTA-Vid: Generalized Test-Time Adaptation for Video Reasoning
summary: Recent video reasoning models have shown strong results on temporal and multimodal understanding, yet they depend on large-scale supervised data and multi-stage training pipelines, making them costly to train and difficult to adapt to new domains. In this work, we leverage the paradigm of Test-Time Reinforcement Learning on video-language data to allow for adapting a pretrained model to incoming video samples at test-time without explicit labels. The proposed test-time adaptation for video approach (TTA-Vid) combines two components that work simultaneously: (1) a test-time adaptation that performs step-by-step reasoning at inference time on multiple frame subsets. We then use a batch-aware frequency-based reward computed across different frame subsets as pseudo ground truth to update the model. It shows that the resulting model trained on a single batch or even a single sample from a dataset, is able to generalize at test-time to the whole dataset and even across datasets. Because the adaptation occurs entirely at test time, our method requires no ground-truth annotations or dedicated training splits. Additionally, we propose a multi-armed bandit strategy for adaptive frame selection that learns to prioritize informative frames, guided by the same reward formulation. Our evaluation shows that TTA-Vid yields consistent improvements across various video reasoning tasks and is able to outperform current state-of-the-art methods trained on large-scale data. This highlights the potential of test-time reinforcement learning for temporal multimodal understanding.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TTA-Vid, a test-time adaptation approach for video reasoning models that can adapt to new video samples without explicit labels, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Current video reasoning models require large-scale supervised data and multi-stage training pipelines, making them costly and difficult to adapt to new domains.
AI.method: TTA-Vid combines test-time adaptation and a multi-armed bandit strategy for adaptive frame selection, using a batch-aware frequency-based reward to update the model.
AI.result: TTA-Vid yields consistent improvements across various video reasoning tasks, outperforming current state-of-the-art methods trained on large-scale data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach highlights the potential of test-time reinforcement learning for temporal multimodal understanding, allowing for efficient adaptation to new video samples without explicit labels.

title: Agentic Tool Use in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models are increasingly being deployed as autonomous agents yet their real world effectiveness depends on reliable tools for information retrieval, computation and external action. Existing studies remain fragmented across tasks, tool types, and training settings, lacking a unified view of how tool-use methods differ and evolve. This paper organizes the literature into three paradigms: prompting as plug-and-play, supervised tool learning and reward-driven tool policy learning, analyzes their methods, strengths and failure modes, reviews the evaluation landscape and highlights key challenges, aiming to address this fragmentation and provide a more structured evolutionary view of agentic tool use.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a unified view of tool-use methods in large language models, categorizing them into three paradigms and analyzing their methods, strengths, and challenges.
AI.motivation: The increasing deployment of large language models as autonomous agents requires reliable tools for information retrieval, computation, and external action, but existing studies are fragmented across tasks, tool types, and training settings.
AI.method: The paper organizes the literature into three paradigms: prompting as plug-and-play, supervised tool learning, and reward-driven tool policy learning, and analyzes their methods, strengths, and failure modes.
AI.result: The paper provides a structured evolutionary view of agentic tool use, highlighting key challenges and the evaluation landscape.
AI.conclusion: The paper aims to address the fragmentation of existing studies on tool-use methods in large language models and provide a unified view of their differences and evolution.

title: KUET at StanceNakba Shared Task: StanceMoE: Mixture-of-Experts Architecture for Stance Detection
comment: Accepted for workshop proceedings of the 15th International Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC'26)
summary: Actor-level stance detection aims to determine an author expressed position toward specific geopolitical actors mentioned or implicated in a text. Although transformer-based models have achieved relatively good performance in stance classification, they typically rely on unified representations that may not sufficiently capture heterogeneous linguistic signals, such as contrastive discourse structures, framing cues, and salient lexical indicators. This motivates the need for adaptive architectures that explicitly model diverse stance-expressive patterns. In this paper, we propose StanceMoE, a context-enhanced Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture built upon a fine-tuned BERT encoder for actor-level stance detection. Our model integrates six expert modules designed to capture complementary linguistic signals, including global semantic orientation, salient lexical cues, clause-level focus, phrase-level patterns, framing indicators, and contrast-driven discourse shifts. A context-aware gating mechanism dynamically weights expert contributions, enabling adaptive routing based on input characteristics. Experiments are conducted on the StanceNakba 2026 Subtask A dataset, comprising 1,401 annotated English texts where the target actor is implicit in the text. StanceMoE achieves a macro-F1 score of 94.26%, outperforming traditional baselines, and alternative BERT-based variants.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes StanceMoE, a Mixture-of-Experts architecture for actor-level stance detection, achieving a macro-F1 score of 94.26% on the StanceNakba 2026 Subtask A dataset.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitation of transformer-based models in capturing heterogeneous linguistic signals for stance classification, and to propose an adaptive architecture that explicitly models diverse stance-expressive patterns.
AI.method: The method proposed is StanceMoE, a context-enhanced Mixture-of-Experts architecture built upon a fine-tuned BERT encoder, which integrates six expert modules and a context-aware gating mechanism.
AI.result: The result is that StanceMoE achieves a macro-F1 score of 94.26% on the StanceNakba 2026 Subtask A dataset, outperforming traditional baselines and alternative BERT-based variants.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that StanceMoE is an effective approach for actor-level stance detection, and it demonstrates the importance of adaptive architectures in capturing diverse stance-expressive patterns.

title: IWP: Token Pruning as Implicit Weight Pruning in Large Vision Language Models
summary: Large Vision Language Models show impressive performance across image and video understanding tasks, yet their computational cost grows rapidly with the number of visual tokens. Existing token pruning methods mitigate this issue through empirical approaches while overlooking the internal mechanism of attention. In this paper, we propose a novel training free token pruning framework grounded in the dual form perspective of attention. We reformulate attention as an implicit linear layer whose weight matrix is the sum of rank 1 outer products, each generated by a single token's key value pair. Token pruning thus reduces to selecting an optimal subset of these rank 1 updates that best approximates the original dual weight matrix. Extending this perspective to standard softmax attention in LVLMs, we derive a novel metric quantifying both a token's information magnitude and information duplication. To efficiently select the subset with the proposed metric, we introduce Progressive Chunked Maximal Marginal Relevance. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves a better trade off between performance and efficiency, while providing another perspective on existing pruning approaches.

AI.tldr: A novel token pruning framework for large vision language models, leveraging the dual form perspective of attention to optimize performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The computational cost of large vision language models grows rapidly with the number of visual tokens, and existing token pruning methods are empirical and overlook the internal mechanism of attention.
AI.method: A training-free token pruning framework grounded in the dual form perspective of attention, reformulating attention as an implicit linear layer and deriving a novel metric for token selection.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves a better trade-off between performance and efficiency, validated through extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a new perspective on token pruning and achieves improved performance and efficiency in large vision language models.

title: When Users Change Their Mind: Evaluating Interruptible Agents in Long-Horizon Web Navigation
summary: As LLM agents transition from short, static problem solving to executing complex, long-horizon tasks in dynamic environments, the ability to handle user interruptions, such as adding requirement or revising goals, during mid-task execution is becoming a core requirement for realistic deployment. However, existing benchmarks largely assume uninterrupted agent behavior or study interruptions only in short, unconstrained language tasks. In this paper, we present the first systematic study of interruptible agents in long-horizon, environmentally grounded web navigation tasks, where actions induce persistent state changes. We formalize three realistic interruption types, including addition, revision, and retraction, and introduce InterruptBench, a benchmark derived from WebArena-Lite that synthesizes high-quality interruption scenarios under strict semantic constraints. Using a unified interruption simulation framework, we evaluate six strong LLM backbones across single- and multi-turn interruption settings, analyzing both their effectiveness in adapting to updated intents and their efficiency in recovering from mid-task changes. Our results show that handling user interruptions effectively and efficiently during long-horizon agentic tasks remains challenging for powerful large-scale LLMs. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/HenryPengZou/InterruptBench.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the ability of LLM agents to handle user interruptions during long-horizon tasks in dynamic environments, presenting a new benchmark and evaluating several strong LLM backbones.
AI.motivation: The growing need for LLM agents to handle complex, long-horizon tasks in dynamic environments where user interruptions are common.
AI.method: The authors formalize three types of interruptions and introduce InterruptBench, a benchmark derived from WebArena-Lite, to evaluate LLM agents' ability to adapt to interruptions.
AI.result: The results show that handling user interruptions effectively and efficiently during long-horizon tasks remains challenging for powerful large-scale LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the need for further research in developing LLM agents that can effectively handle user interruptions during long-horizon tasks.

title: PrivHAR-Bench: A Graduated Privacy Benchmark Dataset for Video-Based Action Recognition
summary: Existing research on privacy-preserving Human Activity Recognition (HAR) typically evaluates methods against a binary paradigm: clear video versus a single privacy transformation. This limits cross-method comparability and obscures the nuanced relationship between privacy strength and recognition utility. We introduce \textit{PrivHAR-Bench}, a multi-tier benchmark dataset designed to standardize the evaluation of the \textit{Privacy-Utility Trade-off} in video-based action recognition. PrivHAR-Bench applies a graduated spectrum of visual privacy transformations: from lightweight spatial obfuscation to cryptographic block permutation, to a curated subset of 15 activity classes selected for human articulation diversity. Each of the 1,932 source videos is distributed across 9 parallel tiers of increasing privacy strength, with additional background-removed variants to isolate the contribution of human motion features from contextual scene bias. We provide lossless frame sequences, per-frame bounding boxes, estimated pose keypoints with joint-level confidence scores, standardized group-based train/test splits, and an evaluation toolkit computing recognition accuracy and privacy metrics. Empirical validation using R3D-18 demonstrates a measurable and interpretable degradation curve across tiers, with within-tier accuracy declining from 88.8\% (clear) to 53.5\% (encrypted, background-removed) and cross-domain accuracy collapsing to 4.8\%, establishing PrivHAR-Bench as a controlled benchmark for comparing privacy-preserving HAR methods under standardized conditions. The dataset, generation pipeline, and evaluation code are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduced PrivHAR-Bench, a benchmark dataset for evaluating privacy-preserving Human Activity Recognition (HAR) methods, enabling standardized comparison of privacy-utility trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing research on privacy-preserving HAR is limited by its binary evaluation approach, making it difficult to compare methods and understand the relationship between privacy strength and recognition utility.
AI.method: PrivHAR-Bench applies a graduated spectrum of visual privacy transformations to 1,932 source videos across 9 tiers of increasing privacy strength, with additional background-removed variants.
AI.result: Empirical validation using R3D-18 shows a measurable and interpretable degradation curve across tiers, with accuracy declining from 88.8% (clear) to 53.5% (encrypted, background-removed).
AI.conclusion: PrivHAR-Bench provides a controlled benchmark for comparing privacy-preserving HAR methods under standardized conditions, facilitating advancements in privacy-preserving HAR research.

title: GPT-NL Public Corpus: A Permissively Licensed, Dutch-First Dataset for LLM Pre-training
summary: We present the GPT-NL Public Corpus, the biggest permissively licensed corpus of Dutch language resources. The GPT-NL Public Corpus contains 21 Dutch-only collections totalling 36B preprocessed Dutch tokens not present in any other LLM pretraining corpus. Additionally, the corpus includes roughly 207B English, 232B Code, and 48B German/Danish tokens taken from existing sets which we further curated for compliance. This corpus includes curated data from large existing corpora like Common Corpus and Common Crawl, as well as newly created Dutch-specific collections. Most newly created Dutch collections consist of content collected in collaboration with organisations or synthetically augmented content. All data is collected and evaluated with the aim of facilitating the creation of (commercial) language models that are lawful, useful and non-harmful. All data included in the GPT-NL Public Corpus is sourced from datasets with permissive licensing and is curated and redistributed under a CC-BY license. The full dataset is publicly available on the Hugging Face Hub.

AI.tldr: The GPT-NL Public Corpus is a large, permissively licensed dataset of Dutch language resources, totaling 36B Dutch tokens and 487B tokens in other languages, designed to facilitate the creation of lawful and useful language models.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind creating the GPT-NL Public Corpus is to provide a resource for training language models that are lawful, useful, and non-harmful, specifically focusing on the Dutch language.
AI.method: The corpus was created by curating data from existing large corpora like Common Corpus and Common Crawl, as well as creating new Dutch-specific collections through collaborations with organizations or synthetic augmentation.
AI.result: The GPT-NL Public Corpus contains 36B preprocessed Dutch tokens, 207B English tokens, 232B Code tokens, and 48B German/Danish tokens, all sourced from permissively licensed datasets and redistributed under a CC-BY license.
AI.conclusion: The GPT-NL Public Corpus is a significant resource for the development of Dutch language models, providing a large and diverse dataset that is publicly available and permissively licensed.

title: An Approach to Enriching Surgical Video Datasets for Fine-Grained Spatial-Temporal Understanding of Vision-Language Models
summary: Surgical video understanding is a crucial prerequisite for advancing Computer-Assisted Surgery. While vision-language models (VLMs) have recently been applied to the surgical domain, existing surgical vision-language datasets lack in capturing and evaluating complex, interleaved spatial-temporal dynamics. Creating large scale datasets that accurately represent fine-grained spatial-temporal relationships in surgical videos is challenging due to costly manual annotations or error-prone generation using large language models. To address this gap, we introduce the SurgSTU-Pipeline, a deterministic generation pipeline featuring temporal and spatial continuity filtering to reliably create surgical datasets for fine-grained spatial-temporal multimodal understanding. Applying this pipeline to publicly available surgical datasets, we create the SurgSTU dataset, comprising 7515 video clips densely extended with 150k fine-grained spatial-temporal question-answer samples. Our comprehensive evaluation shows that while state-of-the-art generalist VLMs struggle in zero-shot settings, their spatial-temporal capabilities can be improved through in-context learning. A fine-tuned VLM on the SurgSTU training dataset achieves highest performance among all spatial-temporal tasks, validating the dataset's efficacy to improve spatial-temporal understanding of VLMs in surgical videos. Code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: A new pipeline for generating surgical datasets with fine-grained spatial-temporal multimodal understanding is proposed, creating the SurgSTU dataset which improves the performance of vision-language models in surgical video understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing surgical vision-language datasets lack capturing and evaluating complex, interleaved spatial-temporal dynamics, and creating large-scale datasets with fine-grained spatial-temporal relationships is challenging due to costly manual annotations or error-prone generation.
AI.method: The SurgSTU-Pipeline, a deterministic generation pipeline featuring temporal and spatial continuity filtering, is introduced to reliably create surgical datasets for fine-grained spatial-temporal multimodal understanding.
AI.result: The SurgSTU dataset, comprising 7515 video clips with 150k fine-grained spatial-temporal question-answer samples, is created and shown to improve the spatial-temporal capabilities of vision-language models through in-context learning and fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SurgSTU-Pipeline and dataset effectively improve the spatial-temporal understanding of vision-language models in surgical videos, validating the dataset's efficacy.

title: Positional Cognitive Specialization: Where Do LLMs Learn To Comprehend and Speak Your Language?
summary: Adapting large language models (LLMs) to new languages is an expensive and opaque process. Understanding how language models acquire new languages and multilingual abilities is key to achieve efficient adaptation. Prior work on multilingual interpretability research focuses primarily on how trained models process multilingual instructions, leaving unexplored the mechanisms through which they acquire new languages during training. We investigate these training dynamics on decoder-only transformers through the lens of two functional cognitive specializations: language perception (input comprehension) and production (output generation). Through experiments on low-resource languages, we demonstrate how perceptual and productive specialization emerges in different regions of a language model by running layer ablation sweeps from the model's input and output directions. Based on the observed specialization patterns, we propose CogSym, a layer-wise heuristic that enables effective adaptation by exclusively fine-tuning a few early and late layers. We show that tuning only the 25% outermost layers achieves downstream task performance within 2-3% deviation from the full fine-tuning baseline. CogSym yields consistent performance with adapter methods such as LoRA, showcasing generalization beyond full fine-tuning. These findings provide insights to better understand how LLMs learn new languages and push toward accessible and inclusive language modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models acquire new languages and proposes a method called CogSym for efficient adaptation to new languages.
AI.motivation: The process of adapting large language models to new languages is expensive and not well understood, and prior work has not explored the mechanisms of language acquisition during training.
AI.method: The authors investigate training dynamics through the lens of language perception and production, and propose CogSym, a layer-wise heuristic that fine-tunes only a few early and late layers.
AI.result: The method achieves downstream task performance within 2-3% deviation from full fine-tuning baseline by tuning only 25% outermost layers, and shows consistent performance with adapter methods.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide insights into how large language models learn new languages and push towards more accessible and inclusive language modeling.

title: HICT: High-precision 3D CBCT reconstruction from a single X-ray
summary: Accurate 3D dental imaging is vital for diagnosis and treatment planning, yet CBCT's high radiation dose and cost limit its accessibility. Reconstructing 3D volumes from a single low-dose panoramic X-ray is a promising alternative but remains challenging due to geometric inconsistencies and limited accuracy. We propose HiCT, a two-stage framework that first generates geometrically consistent multi-view projections from a single panoramic image using a video diffusion model, and then reconstructs high-fidelity CBCT from the projections using a ray-based dynamic attention network and an X-ray sampling strategy. To support this, we built XCT, a large-scale dataset combining public CBCT data with 500 paired PX-CBCT cases. Extensive experiments show that HiCT achieves state-of-the-art performance, delivering accurate and geometrically consistent reconstructions for clinical use.

AI.tldr: Proposed HiCT, a two-stage framework for reconstructing 3D dental images from low-dose panoramic X-rays, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Accurate 3D dental imaging is crucial but CBCT's high radiation dose and cost limit accessibility, making reconstruction from single low-dose panoramic X-rays a promising alternative.
AI.method: HiCT uses a two-stage framework: generating multi-view projections from a single panoramic image via a video diffusion model, then reconstructing CBCT using a ray-based dynamic attention network and X-ray sampling strategy.
AI.result: HiCT achieved state-of-the-art performance, delivering accurate and geometrically consistent reconstructions for clinical use.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HiCT framework offers a promising solution for accurate 3D dental imaging from low-dose panoramic X-rays, addressing limitations of current CBCT methods.

title: Phase transition on a context-sensitive random language model with short range interactions
summary: Since the random language model was proposed by E. DeGiuli [Phys. Rev. Lett. 122, 128301], language models have been investigated intensively from the viewpoint of statistical mechanics. Recently, the existence of a Berezinskii--Kosterlitz--Thouless transition was numerically demonstrated in models with long-range interactions between symbols. In statistical mechanics, it has long been known that long-range interactions can induce phase transitions. Therefore, it has remained unclear whether phase transitions observed in language models originate from genuinely linguistic properties that are absent in conventional spin models. In this study, we construct a random language model with short-range interactions and numerically investigate its statistical properties. Our model belongs to the class of context-sensitive grammars in the Chomsky hierarchy and allows explicit reference to contexts. We find that a phase transition occurs even when the model refers only to contexts whose length remains constant with respect to the sentence length. This result indicates that finite-temperature phase transitions in language models are genuinely induced by the intrinsic nature of language, rather than by long-range interactions.

AI.tldr: The study investigates whether phase transitions in language models are due to linguistic properties or long-range interactions, finding that phase transitions occur even with short-range interactions.
AI.motivation: To determine if phase transitions in language models originate from linguistic properties or long-range interactions.
AI.method: Constructing a random language model with short-range interactions and analyzing its statistical properties.
AI.result: A phase transition occurs even when the model refers only to contexts of constant length with respect to sentence length.
AI.conclusion: Finite-temperature phase transitions in language models are genuinely induced by the intrinsic nature of language, not by long-range interactions.

title: Multimodal Language Models Cannot Spot Spatial Inconsistencies
summary: Spatial consistency is a fundamental property of the visual world and a key requirement for models that aim to understand physical reality. Despite recent advances, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often struggle to reason about 3D geometry across multiple views. Rather than asking models to describe scene attributes, we introduce a more challenging task: given two views of the same scene, identify the object that violates 3D motion consistency. We propose a simple and scalable method for generating realistic, spatially inconsistent image pairs from multi-view scenes, enabling systematic evaluation of this capability. Our results show that state-of-the-art MLLMs significantly underperform human observers and exhibit substantial variability across different scene attributes, revealing a fragile and incomplete understanding of 3D structure. We hope our findings underscore the need for approaches that develop a more deeply grounded understanding of the physical world.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a task to identify objects violating 3D motion consistency in image pairs and proposes a method to generate realistic, spatially inconsistent image pairs, revealing state-of-the-art multimodal large language models' limitations in understanding 3D structure.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the struggle of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in reasoning about 3D geometry across multiple views and to develop a more grounded understanding of the physical world.
AI.method: The method proposed is a simple and scalable approach for generating realistic, spatially inconsistent image pairs from multi-view scenes.
AI.result: State-of-the-art MLLMs significantly underperform human observers and exhibit substantial variability across different scene attributes, revealing a fragile and incomplete understanding of 3D structure.
AI.conclusion: The findings underscore the need for approaches that develop a more deeply grounded understanding of the physical world.

title: Dual Optimal: Make Your LLM Peer-like with Dignity
summary: Current aligned language models exhibit a dual failure mode we term the Evasive Servant: they sycophantically validate flawed user beliefs while deflecting responsibility with boilerplate disclaimers. We propose the Dignified Peer framework, which counters servility with anti-sycophancy and trustworthiness, and mitigates evasiveness through empathy and creativity. Realizing this agent requires overcoming significant challenges in data supervision, objective collapse, and evaluation bias. We address these issues by introducing the PersonaKnob dataset which features a compositional partial order structure of multiple persona preference. This data is utilized alongside a tolerant constrained Lagrangian DPO algorithm that dynamically balances all persona dimensions to prevent behavioral collapse. Additionally, we employ a psychometrically calibrated Item Response Theory evaluation protocol to disentangle latent model persona capability from confounders like judge biases. Extensive empirical studies demonstrate that our approach successfully build a LLM agent with both dignity and peer.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework called Dignified Peer to address the 'Evasive Servant' failure mode in aligned language models, which validates flawed user beliefs and deflects responsibility. The framework aims to promote anti-sycophancy, trustworthiness, empathy, and creativity.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address the dual failure mode of aligned language models, termed the Evasive Servant, where they validate flawed user beliefs and deflect responsibility with boilerplate disclaimers.
AI.method: The authors propose the Dignified Peer framework, which utilizes the PersonaKnob dataset with a compositional partial order structure of multiple persona preferences. They also employ a tolerant constrained Lagrangian DPO algorithm and a psychometrically calibrated Item Response Theory evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Extensive empirical studies demonstrate that the proposed approach successfully builds a LLM agent with both dignity and peer characteristics, mitigating the Evasive Servant failure mode.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the Dignified Peer framework is effective in promoting anti-sycophancy, trustworthiness, empathy, and creativity in aligned language models, addressing the issues of servility and evasiveness.

title: Revisiting Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval with Pre-Trained Vision Transformers
summary: Building on existing approaches, we revisit Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval, a task that consists of iteratively retrieving images containing objects of a class-of-interest, specified by a user-provided query. Starting from a large unlabeled image collection, the aim is to rapidly identify diverse instances of an object category relying solely on the initial query and the user's Relevance Feedback, with no prior labels. The retrieval process is formulated as a binary classification task, where the system continuously learns to distinguish between relevant and non-relevant images to the query, through iterative user interaction. This interaction is guided by an Active Learning loop: at each iteration, the system selects informative samples for user annotation, thereby refining the retrieval performance. This task is particularly challenging in multi-object datasets, where the object of interest may occupy only a small region of the image within a complex, cluttered scene. Unlike object-centered settings where global descriptors often suffice, multi-object images require more adapted, localized descriptors. In this work, we formulate and revisit the Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval task by leveraging pre-trained ViT representations, and addressing key design questions, including which object instances to consider in an image, what form the annotations should take, how Active Selection should be applied, and which representation strategies best capture the object's features. We compare several representation strategies across multi-object datasets highlighting trade-offs between capturing the global context and focusing on fine-grained local object details. Our results offer practical insights for the design of effective interactive retrieval pipelines based on Active Learning for object class retrieval.

AI.tldr: This paper revisits Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval, a task that iteratively retrieves images containing specific objects based on user queries and feedback. It explores using pre-trained ViT representations and addresses key design questions for effective interactive retrieval.
AI.motivation: The task is challenging, especially in multi-object datasets where objects may occupy small regions within complex scenes. Existing approaches often rely on global descriptors, which may not suffice for multi-object images.
AI.method: The authors formulate the retrieval process as a binary classification task, using an Active Learning loop to select informative samples for user annotation. They explore various representation strategies, including leveraging pre-trained ViT representations, to capture object features.
AI.result: The results provide practical insights for designing effective interactive retrieval pipelines based on Active Learning for object class retrieval, highlighting trade-offs between capturing global context and focusing on fine-grained local object details.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers a comprehensive revisit of Human-in-the-Loop Object Retrieval, emphasizing the importance of adapted, localized descriptors for multi-object images and providing guidance on designing effective interactive retrieval systems.

title: Multimodal Analysis of State-Funded News Coverage of the Israel-Hamas War on YouTube Shorts
summary: YouTube Shorts have become central to news consumption on the platform, yet research on how geopolitical events are represented in this format remains limited. To address this gap, we present a multimodal pipeline that combines automatic transcription, aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA), and semantic scene classification. The pipeline is first assessed for feasibility and then applied to analyze short-form coverage of the Israel-Hamas war by state-funded outlets. Using over 2,300 conflict-related Shorts and more than 94,000 visual frames, we systematically examine war reporting across major international broadcasters. Our findings reveal that the sentiment expressed in transcripts regarding specific aspects differs across outlets and over time, whereas scene-type classifications reflect visual cues consistent with real-world events. Notably, smaller domain-adapted models outperform large transformers and even LLMs for sentiment analysis, underscoring the value of resource-efficient approaches for humanities research. The pipeline serves as a template for other short-form platforms, such as TikTok and Instagram, and demonstrates how multimodal methods, combined with qualitative interpretation, can characterize sentiment patterns and visual cues in algorithmically driven video environments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a multimodal pipeline to analyze YouTube Shorts' coverage of geopolitical events, applying it to state-funded outlets' coverage of the Israel-Hamas war.
AI.motivation: To address the limited research on how geopolitical events are represented in YouTube Shorts, a new format that has become central to news consumption on the platform.
AI.method: A multimodal pipeline combining automatic transcription, aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA), and semantic scene classification was developed and applied to over 2,300 conflict-related Shorts and 94,000 visual frames.
AI.result: The findings reveal differences in sentiment expressed in transcripts across outlets and over time, and scene-type classifications reflecting visual cues consistent with real-world events. Smaller domain-adapted models outperformed large transformers and LLMs for sentiment analysis.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline provides a template for analyzing short-form platforms like TikTok and Instagram, demonstrating the value of multimodal methods in characterizing sentiment patterns and visual cues in algorithmically driven video environments.

title: DVGT-2: Vision-Geometry-Action Model for Autonomous Driving at Scale
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving has evolved from the conventional paradigm based on sparse perception into vision-language-action (VLA) models, which focus on learning language descriptions as an auxiliary task to facilitate planning. In this paper, we propose an alternative Vision-Geometry-Action (VGA) paradigm that advocates dense 3D geometry as the critical cue for autonomous driving. As vehicles operate in a 3D world, we think dense 3D geometry provides the most comprehensive information for decision-making. However, most existing geometry reconstruction methods (e.g., DVGT) rely on computationally expensive batch processing of multi-frame inputs and cannot be applied to online planning. To address this, we introduce a streaming Driving Visual Geometry Transformer (DVGT-2), which processes inputs in an online manner and jointly outputs dense geometry and trajectory planning for the current frame. We employ temporal causal attention and cache historical features to support on-the-fly inference. To further enhance efficiency, we propose a sliding-window streaming strategy and use historical caches within a certain interval to avoid repetitive computations. Despite the faster speed, DVGT-2 achieves superior geometry reconstruction performance on various datasets. The same trained DVGT-2 can be directly applied to planning across diverse camera configurations without fine-tuning, including closed-loop NAVSIM and open-loop nuScenes benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new Vision-Geometry-Action (VGA) paradigm for end-to-end autonomous driving, introducing a streaming Driving Visual Geometry Transformer (DVGT-2) that efficiently processes inputs online to output dense 3D geometry and trajectory planning.
AI.motivation: The authors are motivated by the limitations of existing geometry reconstruction methods, which are computationally expensive and not suitable for online planning, and they aim to provide a more efficient and effective solution for autonomous driving.
AI.method: The authors propose a streaming Driving Visual Geometry Transformer (DVGT-2) that processes inputs in an online manner, using temporal causal attention and caching historical features to support on-the-fly inference, and a sliding-window streaming strategy to enhance efficiency.
AI.result: DVGT-2 achieves superior geometry reconstruction performance on various datasets, and can be directly applied to planning across diverse camera configurations without fine-tuning, demonstrating its efficiency and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the proposed VGA paradigm and DVGT-2 model provide a more efficient and effective solution for end-to-end autonomous driving, leveraging dense 3D geometry as a critical cue for decision-making.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Variational Reward Factorization via Probabilistic Preference Bases for LLM Personalization
summary: Reward factorization personalizes large language models (LLMs) by decomposing rewards into shared basis functions and user-specific weights. Yet, existing methods estimate user weights from scarce data in isolation and as deterministic points, leading to inaccurate and unreliable inference. We introduce Variational Reward Factorization (VRF), an uncertainty-aware framework that represents each user's preferences as a variational distribution in a shared preference space. VRF infers user distributions via a variational encoder, derives weights through Wasserstein distance matching with shared probabilistic bases, and downweights uncertain estimates through a variance-attenuated loss. On three benchmarks, VRF outperforms all baselines across seen and unseen users, few-shot scenarios, and varying uncertainty levels, with gains extending to downstream alignment.

AI.tldr: Introduced Variational Reward Factorization (VRF), an uncertainty-aware framework that personalizes large language models by representing user preferences as variational distributions, outperforming baselines in multiple scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing reward factorization methods for personalizing large language models have limitations, including inaccurate and unreliable inference due to estimating user weights from scarce data in isolation and as deterministic points.
AI.method: Variational Reward Factorization (VRF) framework, which represents user preferences as variational distributions in a shared preference space, infers user distributions via a variational encoder, and derives weights through Wasserstein distance matching with shared probabilistic bases.
AI.result: VRF outperforms all baselines across seen and unseen users, few-shot scenarios, and varying uncertainty levels, with gains extending to downstream alignment on three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Variational Reward Factorization framework effectively addresses the limitations of existing methods, providing a more accurate and reliable approach to personalizing large language models.

title: Multicentric thrombus segmentation using an attention-based recurrent network with gradual modality dropout
summary: Detecting and delineating tiny targets in 3D brain scans is a central yet under-addressed challenge in medical imaging.In ischemic stroke, for instance, the culprit thrombus is small, low-contrast, and variably expressed across modalities(e.g., susceptibility-weighted T2 blooming, diffusion restriction on DWI/ADC), while real-world multi-center dataintroduce domain shifts, anisotropy, and frequent missing sequences. We introduce a methodology that couples an attention-based recurrent segmentation network (UpAttLLSTM), a training schedule that progressively increases the difficulty of hetero-modal learning, with gradual modality dropout, UpAttLLSTM aggregates context across slices via recurrent units (2.5D) and uses attention gates to fuse complementary cues across available sequences, making it robust to anisotropy and class imbalance. Gradual modality dropout systematically simulates site heterogeneity,noise, and missing modalities during training, acting as both augmentation and regularization to improve multi-center generalization. On a monocentric cohort, our approach detects thrombi in >90% of cases with a Dice score of 0.65. In a multi-center setting with missing modalities, it achieves-80% detection with a Dice score around 0.35. Beyond stroke, the proposed methodology directly transfers to other small-lesion tasks in 3D medical imaging where targets are scarce, subtle, and modality-dependent

AI.tldr: A novel methodology for detecting tiny targets in 3D brain scans is proposed, leveraging an attention-based recurrent segmentation network and a training schedule with gradual modality dropout, achieving high detection accuracy in ischemic stroke and other small-lesion tasks.
AI.motivation: Detecting and delineating tiny targets in 3D brain scans is a central challenge in medical imaging, particularly in ischemic stroke where the culprit thrombus is small, low-contrast, and variably expressed across modalities.
AI.method: The proposed methodology couples an attention-based recurrent segmentation network (UpAttLLSTM) with a training schedule that progressively increases the difficulty of hetero-modal learning and gradual modality dropout.
AI.result: The approach achieves >90% detection of thrombi with a Dice score of 0.65 on a monocentric cohort and ~80% detection with a Dice score around 0.35 in a multi-center setting with missing modalities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methodology demonstrates robust performance in detecting tiny targets in 3D brain scans, particularly in ischemic stroke, and directly transfers to other small-lesion tasks in 3D medical imaging.

title: Narrative Fingerprints: Multi-Scale Author Identification via Novelty Curve Dynamics
summary: We test whether authors have characteristic "fingerprints" in the information-theoretic novelty curves of their published works. Working with two corpora -- Books3 (52,796 books, 759 qualifying authors) and PG-19 (28,439 books, 1,821 qualifying authors) -- we find that authorial voice leaves measurable traces in how novelty unfolds across a text. The signal is multi-scale: at book level, scalar dynamics (mean novelty, speed, volume, circuitousness) identify 43% of authors significantly above chance; at chapter level, SAX motif patterns in sliding windows achieve 30x-above-chance attribution, far exceeding the scalar features that dominate at book level. These signals are complementary, not redundant. We show that the fingerprint is partly confounded with genre but persists within-genre for approximately one-quarter of authors. Classical authors (Twain, Austen, Kipling) show fingerprints comparable in strength to modern authors, suggesting the phenomenon is not an artifact of contemporary publishing conventions.

AI.tldr: The study finds that authors have unique 'fingerprints' in the information-theoretic novelty curves of their works, allowing for significant author identification.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether authors have characteristic patterns in the novelty curves of their published works.
AI.method: The study uses two corpora, Books3 and PG-19, and analyzes the novelty curves at both book and chapter levels using scalar dynamics and SAX motif patterns.
AI.result: The study finds that authorial voice can be identified with 43% accuracy at book level and 30x-above-chance attribution at chapter level, with the signals being complementary rather than redundant.
